title: Cash Flow Projection and Business Financial Planning
summary: Erin Stetzer walked participants through her company's cash flow projection methodology, covering the philosophical framework for generating and managing money in a building business. She shared a detailed cash flow spreadsheet tool showing how to forecast gross revenue by project type, track expenses by category, and project net cash available as a business tool. The session opened into rich discussion on business models, pricing structures, org charts, hiring decisions, trade payments, client communication, and draw schedules.

teachings.concept: The Four-Stage Framework for Generating and Growing Cash
teachings.explanation: Erin outlined a progression from generating cash to making more money: (1) Identify who you can help, (2) Design the pathway by building skills and doing the work, (3) Master your craft to differentiate yourself and solve bigger problems, and (4) Sell your value clearly. This framework positions cash generation as a natural byproduct of focused skill-building and value creation.
teachings.quote: The way you generate cash, or at least what has worked for us, is you figure out step number one, which is who can I help? That's why businesses are around, you know, see a need, fill a need.

teachings.concept: Cash Flow Predictability as a Decision-Making Tool
teachings.explanation: Rather than viewing cash flow as a stressful obligation, Erin frames it as a tool that creates predictability so owners can plan their next three, six, nine, and twelve months of moves. A cash flow projection spreadsheet converts revenue and expense data into a forward-looking snapshot that enables confident hiring and investment decisions.
teachings.quote: You can make decisions on your next moves or your next three moves when you can capture predictability in three months, six months, nine months, 12 months.

teachings.concept: Money as a Tool, Not Just an Outcome
teachings.explanation: Once a business achieves predictable cash flow, money becomes a lever to invest in people, invest in time savings, and leverage opportunities. The mental shift from 'I need to make a dollar' to 'I have tools to work with' marks a maturation point in business ownership.
teachings.quote: We've gone from I need to make a dollar to I have tools. There's money here for me to work with.

teachings.concept: Separating Business Units in Financial Reporting
teachings.explanation: Erin recommends tracking new construction and remodeling revenue in separate line items within the cash flow spreadsheet. Because margins differ significantly between business units, separating them reveals which units are healthy and which need attention, preventing blended numbers from hiding problems.
teachings.quote: The reason I think that is important when you have different business units is you'll discover some things about each of these business units that are either very positive or need work.

teachings.concept: Owner Pay as a Non-Negotiable Expense
teachings.explanation: Erin emphasizes that owners must include their own salary as a real line item in the budget and cash flow projections. Failing to account for owner compensation distorts the true health of the business and leads to unsustainable operations.
teachings.quote: Don't forget yourself. I learned that lesson early on. I was paying everybody else and my accountants like, Erin, listen, you got to pay attention here because you're not in the business of doing things for free.

teachings.concept: Hiring Timing Based on Cash Flow Health
teachings.explanation: The cash flow projection reveals when monthly income begins declining due to project wind-downs, signaling when new business must be generated. Hiring should happen when the company is financially healthy, not when it is stressed, so that the owner can make reliable commitments to new team members.
teachings.quote: You want to hire someone... you can calculate the health of the company when you have a snapshot of monthly cash flow. You can also figure out where it starts to go down because new business will be important.

teachings.concept: Mastering a Specific Craft Creates Disproportionate Value
teachings.explanation: Using the example of a young oil rig worker being flown to Dubai because he was the only person who could fix a specific component, Erin illustrates that becoming the go-to expert for a niche problem commands premium compensation and creates irreplaceable value. Builders should identify their unique superpower and develop it relentlessly.
teachings.quote: He's the one guy that knows how to fix the very little thing in that rig. Be that person that people go to.

teachings.concept: The Bridge Role Between Field and Finance
teachings.explanation: In growing construction companies, there is a communication gap between project managers who own field knowledge and the money team who needs structured information. A 'bridge person' translates field realities into formal modification and change documentation without requiring PMs to create paperwork, and without requiring finance staff to understand construction details.
teachings.quote: I don't want the project managers trying to put together a formal document. All I need is the information. So then what happens is the bridge person knows what's happening in the field and the bridge person can also explain this to the money person.

actionable_advice: Build a cash flow projection spreadsheet that separates gross revenue by business unit (e.g., new construction vs. remodeling) and tracks expenses across payroll, marketing, and operating categories by month.
Include your own salary as a real payroll expense in all cash flow models so the spreadsheet reflects true business health.
Review cash flow weekly with your financial team member, at minimum, to stay within arm's length of where money is moving.
For cost-plus projects, study monthly draw patterns as a percentage of total project cost and use those historical percentages to project future draws accurately.
Include post-close warranty costs in the original project budget by asking each trade what it would cost to return for a touch-up or adjustment after move-in.
Require a 10% upfront deposit from clients and hold it through the end of the project as a float to cover expenses between draws, effectively letting the client self-fund a line of credit.
Bill twice per month (first and fifteenth) for cash clients to smooth out cash flow volatility rather than billing all at once.

When a project manager is not getting client responses via email, require them to pick up the phone instead, as email communication with clients is often ineffective and should be reserved for documentation.
When considering whether to take on additional projects, sign the contracts first and solve the staffing challenge second rather than declining work out of fear of capacity.
Work backwards from a target net profit percentage (e.g., 10%) when building your overhead budget rather than calculating overhead first and hoping profit remains.
client_challenges: Ryan (Providence Homes) is at a growth inflection point with $7 million in projected revenue over 18 months and is unsure whether to hire another project manager or back-office support first, while also trying to determine if his overhead-to-revenue ratio is healthy.

David noted that in his market, showing a markup above 15% on large projects creates client skepticism, making it difficult to price overhead staff into the markup the way some other business models do.

Brett (design-build) is four years into construction and struggling to define the division of responsibility between his pricing team and project managers for modifications and change orders, particularly given limited construction expertise on the pricing side.
A participant asked via chat where trade payments should be accounted for in the cash flow spreadsheet, since the template shown only reflected internal team costs.
Lauren asked how Erin factors post-close warranty costs into her cash flow models, and whether those reserved dollars are kept separate from operating cash.

Brooke raised the question of how written documentation and email trails are maintained for legal or dispute purposes when project managers handle all client communication independently without copying ownership.

Ryan shared that he has been operating on very thin margins and is unsure what a healthy operating cost percentage looks like relative to total revenue, having not yet established a reliable salary for himself.
notable_quotes: On mastering your craft: 'Instead of being in the pool of hundreds of thousands of people, we're in a much smaller pool over here when we master our craft.'

On owner involvement: 'It's really important for owners to be out in the dirt because that's where relationships are made. Our business continues to grow because the owner is still active in the game.'

On early-stage financial vigilance: 'When I first started the company, I was in the bank account every day. I was on paying the bills. I knew exactly where everything was going. That was the cadence every day.'
On AI and client communication: 'This laziness around reporting, I see it. I see it already. If you need a decision from them, that's what they need. Not a two-hour meeting transcript.'
On early lessons in profitability: 'Do you know the first custom home that I built? I think I made $42. I'm not kidding you. 42 bucks on like a million and a half home.'
On urgency as a competitive advantage: 'Not everybody can handle that. And not everybody can move through it. But we can. And you can. And that's repetition.'
